detailed_analysis: The headline structural finding is agentic_tool_spec.py. The module has grown to 721 lines, well past the 500-line module threshold, and is now a true God Module with mixed responsibilities: (1) defining the AgenticToolSpec / CustomizationTypeIO / FileLayout data model, (2) a generic rules-IO factory, (3) a generic mcp_server-IO factory, (4) four hand-rolled per-tool builder functions, each 80–140 lines, and (5) the public registry assembler. The per-tool builders are not just data — they each define five inner closures that mostly just thread artifact_path/prior_canonical through to the real IO module. There is also a duplicate-truth problem about the capability matrix. The supported set of customization_types for a tool is encoded twice: once in AgenticToolSpec.config_dir_keys and once in AgenticToolSpec.io. supported_customization_types reads only from io, but tool_status.ToolStatusTracker iterates config_dir_keys. If those two dicts ever drift, the IO will exist but the tool will report 'available' without ever probing that root, or vice-versa. There is no __post_init__ invariant. The per-tool capability is also informally encoded in dialect parameters. Each McpServerDialect literal in _build_*_spec is a 6–12-line bag of optional fields — a Data Clumps / Primitive Obsession smell. The _mcp_server_io factory has a more subtle defect: the dialect parameter type is annotated Any | None. The tests on this branch under-pin the new capabilities. test_agentic_tool_spec.py asserts only the presence of mcp_server IO and the right config-key strings. It does not assert: the dialect parameters; the map_key_path (the difference between claude's ('mcpServers',) and codex's ('mcp_servers',) and opencode's ('mcp',) is invisible); the file_format ('json' vs 'toml'); round-trip behavior for any rules or mcp_server cell. tool_status.py is well-scoped, but its two methods ensure_roots and _probe_tool_roots contain near-identical SharedKeyedMapLayout branches that compute parent = resolved.parent then act on it — duplicated logic, two places to change. The isinstance switch-on-type is also a Feature Envy / polymorphism smell.

findings.problem: Module is 721 lines, 44% over the 500-line threshold, and mixes (1) the AgenticToolSpec data model, (2) three IO-factory functions, (3) four per-tool registrations each containing 5-7 inner closures, and (4) the public registry assembler. Imported by discovery, adoption, rendering, sync, and tool_status.

Single function is 136 lines — over 3x the 40-line threshold. _build_claude_spec (108) and _build_codex_spec (116) are the same shape. The inner closures are pure boilerplate. The McpServerDialect literal alone is 14 lines of keyword arguments.

findings.variant: Capability matrix declared in two dicts without invariant check
findings.problem: The set of customization_types a tool supports is encoded twice — once in config_dir_keys, once in io. supported_customization_types reads only from io, but tool_status iterates config_dir_keys. There is no __post_init__ invariant asserting set(config_dir_keys.keys()) == set(io.keys()). With the v0.5 expansion adding rules + mcp_server slots in two places, drift risk doubled.

findings.variant: Data Clumps + Primitive Obsession on McpServerDialect
findings.problem: The per-tool MCP capability surface is a free-form bag of optional dialect fields. No per-capability enum, no per-tool subclass, no central comparison table. Anyone wanting to know 'does opencode support enabled-fields' has to read the literal.

findings.variant: Missing / weakened type annotation on a public-shape factory
findings.problem: dialect is typed Any | None to dodge a top-level import of McpServerDialect. A typo in a per-tool dialect field would not be caught by mypy.

findings.variant: Unused parameter accepted to satisfy ParseFn signature
findings.problem: Multiple inner closures accept artifact_path and artifact_root because the ParseFn protocol requires them, but silently drop one or both.

findings.variant: isinstance switch on FileLayout subclass + duplicated SharedKeyedMapLayout branch
findings.problem: The FileLayout Protocol was designed precisely so callers could ask the layout how to behave. Instead, ToolStatusTracker uses two isinstance checks. The two branches duplicate the resolved.parent derivation. A third FileLayout subclass will require editing both methods.

findings.variant: Missing config-key guard for non-SharedKeyedMapLayout branch
findings.problem: The SharedKeyedMapLayout branch tolerates a missing config_key. The SingleFileLayout / directory_skill branch does a direct self.config[config_key] — raising KeyError if the key is missing. The asymmetric tolerance is undocumented.

findings.variant: Assertions check presence but not differentiating per-tool dialect parameters
findings.problem: The tests cover the v0.5 capability expansion in name only. They verify that an mcp_server IO exists on each tool, but the load-bearing per-tool difference — which keyed-map path is used (('mcpServers',) vs ('mcp_servers',) vs ('mcp',)), which file_format, which McpServerDialect fields differ — is never asserted. A regression that swaps claude's map_key_path to ('mcp_servers',) would pass this entire test file.

findings.variant: No round-trip test for rules or mcp_server cells
findings.problem: Only two round-trips through the registry are exercised. The v0.5 additions — rules (3 tools), mcp_server (3 tools), slash_command (3 tools) — are not exercised end-to-end through the registry's IO bundle. 9 of 15 currently-registered cells untested.

verdict_summary: No CRITICAL findings — security is sound. But agentic_tool_spec.py has crossed into God-Module territory (721 lines, four 100+-line per-tool builders), encodes the capability matrix in two non-cross-checked dicts, hides per-tool MCP differences in a Data-Clumps dialect bag, and the v0.5 tests pin only the names of new capabilities — not the per-tool dialect/map_key_path/format that differentiates them.
